title: Is a European Single Market for Energy Such a Good Idea?
gist: Brigitte Granville argues that while textbook microeconomics favors a more integrated European energy market, deeper integration could be a bad deal for low-cost low-carbon electricity producers and, by creating free riders, could trigger a profound political backlash.

insights.claim: Although less energy-market fragmentation would be better for Europe as a whole per textbook microeconomics, it would not be a good deal for countries producing low-carbon electricity at minimal marginal cost.
insights.speaker: Brigitte Granville
insights.so_what: Watch low-cost producer nations (e.g., nuclear/hydro-heavy) as likely political blockers of EU energy-market integration.
insights.quote: that does not mean it would be a good deal for countries producing low-carbon electricity at minimal marginal cost

insights.claim: If deeper energy integration creates free riders, the resulting political backlash could be profound, threatening the integration project itself.
insights.speaker: Brigitte Granville
insights.so_what: Political-backlash risk makes EU energy-integration timelines less reliable than the economic case suggests.
insights.quote: if more integration creates free riders, the political backlash could be profound

insights.claim: This year's supply disruptions further confirm the need to phase out fossil fuels both to mitigate climate change and to strengthen energy security, independent of the US-Iran peace process outcome.
insights.speaker: Brigitte Granville
insights.so_what: Recurring supply shocks reinforce the structural case for European energy-transition and security investment.
insights.quote: The crisis is further confirmation of the need to phase out fossil fuels, both to mitigate climate change and to strengthen energy security.
insights.locator: Lede, 'LONDON—Whatever happens with the US-Iran peace process'

insights.claim: To reverse the precipitous decline in its share of global GDP this century, Europe must lower its energy costs, given its heavy dependence on imported energy.
insights.speaker: Brigitte Granville
insights.so_what: European competitiveness is framed as energy-cost-bound, a key macro lever for relative EU equity performance.
insights.quote: To address the precipitous decline in its share of global GDP this century, Europe must lower its energy costs.
insights.locator: Lede, 'But for Europe, which remains heavily dependent'
